cases in the journey after they bought, we don't want there to be friction. We want the easy button. We want them to be able to get results quickly. If you look at e-com right now, how many people are in e-com? Nobody's here in e-com? Let me see it in the chat. We got e-com people, right? 70% of online shopping carts are abandoned. For the course creators out there, right? Only three to 15 people get results from programs. And there's a massive amount of people that buy programs and never even start. And if you're coaches, we get people that are complaining that they didn't get the results because there's expectations that are mismatched all the time. So when you start thinking about this, it takes 12 positive experiences to make up for one negative one. It's expensive, it's hard, it takes a long time. And then we have what I call stranger danger. This is where you spend all your time focusing on that funnel. Now, I believe in the hour-less funnel too, right? I'm all for it, right? But here's the thing, and I think many of us are guilty about this. Most business will do anything to get a stranger in the door. And once they say yes, the business moves on to the next stranger. How many people are guilty of that? Let's be honest. Show of hands, right? So the best way to compete and grow your business is to get results for your customers. What I want you to do is I want you to flip that funnel. I want you to put your focus and your attention on the journey after they say yes. Because there's an amazing thing that'll happen. When you prioritize your existing customers over strangers, they're going to love you. Remember that testimonial that Russell showed of Alex Hormozzi and Brandon and Katelyn? Like, they got results. They love him. They will tell everybody about that, right? Here's the other thing, it's seven times more expensive to acquire a new customer than he's to keep a current one. We're all talking about how to get our class down. Here's a great strategy. Get our existing customers to love us more. And here's the other thing. On that road, loyal customers worth up to ten times as much as their first purchase. We've heard so many people today talk about, I bought Russell's book, and then I came into this, and then I came into this. Now I'm an atlas, right? We hear that all the time. We get compounding customer growth when we focus on this. Customers bring in more customers. So I want to give you some strategies of how to do this. Is that cool? Yes. All right. So I want you to shift your perspective. I want you to think about your customers as if you were your customer. I want you to put on their glasses. I want you to look through their eyes. I want you to see your business, the journey that they're going on, the way they see it. Because most of us are really focused and really selfish. We're looking at what we need to do, how we save money, how we make it easier for our team. But we need to look at how we make it easier for our customers. So there's four key variables that we need to think about, right? The four key variables we need to think about are the attitudes of our customers. That's what they're thinking and they're expecting. We need to think about the behaviors, the things that they're doing or that we're asking them to do to get those results. We have to think about the friction, the things that are getting in their way, the things that are stopping them from actually having some success. And then we have to find ways to induce momentum. How do we give them a boost? How do we make things easier? So we have a program that we call the Kinetic Customer Formule. We teach this because we want customers not to have potential energy that are sitting on the fence waiting to do something. We want customers that have kinetic energy. They're active. They're engaged. And so this is the formula. I'm going to give it to you right now. It's attitudes plus behaviors induced and boosted by momentum by instruction gets you to that radical result. It looks like this on a graph. You have time, you have results, and you have friction points, and you have momentum. We want to keep people above the results line, especially as you go across time. We need to use momentum to boost them. So how do we do this? We map their journey. We literally look at every single touch point that they go through on their journey. It sounds complicated. It's not. But it's emotionally and it's draining because as you start to look at what they go through, you're going to start to feel a little bit of their pain. And you're probably going to feel some embarrassment if I'm being honest. I bet clients go through workshops with us where they end up crying at the end of the experience because they feel badly for how they have potentially mistreated their customers. And so it's okay because we can fix that. So there's five critical questions that you have to ask as you're going through this process. The first one, and by the way, I put the glasses on there as a reminder for me, you do all of this as if you were them. So what is the customer doing? It's like I'm receiving an email. Not you're sending an email. It's from what they're seeing, what they're receiving. So what is the customer doing? Then we ask, what are they actually thinking or expecting? We look at what they're using. Are they using their cell phone? Are they using a computer? Are they using a card catalog in a library? Whatever it might be. Who are they interacting with? And last and most important, how do they feel? The emotions are the most important thing. And when you look at your journey, I promise you, textbook case, you're going to look like this unbelievable roller coaster ride of emotions as people are going through the process with you. And we want to make that a little bit more fun. We want to make it a little bit more exciting. And we want to make it a whole lot more choreographed, getting them to that final result. So we're going to eliminate friction and we're going to boost momentum. So I don't have a whole lot of time, so I'm going to give you my favorite strategies so that you can start doing this immediately in your business. The first one sounds so basic, but it's the one that everyone seems to miss. You have to set and manage expectations. Because here's the thing that we all, we kind of miss. If you don't set them and manage them, customers still have them. So why let them have an expectation that you can't meet? We have to set and manage those expectations so that we can help them get those results. Featers taught us that the scene changes are really important. We lose people in transitions. How about when you onboard people? That's a big transition. How about when they first come into your world, even in the sales process, big transition, as you start to build that relationship? And we want to make those transitions as seamless, as smooth, as comfortable as possible. We want to look at the time to first value. We want to get people a win as close to their decision of yes as possible. And then we want to have a win cycle. We want them to keep winning. So what is a win? It's a perception of positive momentum. It's a perception of value. It's not that they got the ultimate result in the whole transformation. They feel that they're making progress and they get excited about that. And that helps them get to the next step and the next step. Here's another one that people argue with me about. I want you to stop constantly over-delivering. This whole under-promise over-deliver makes me so angry because here's the thing. When everything is over-delivery, it's not over-delivery anymore. It's expected. So we think we're doing a service to our customers by over-delivering all the time, and we're actually overwhelming them, overloading them, and we're not impressing them after the first couple of times. They expect it. So I want you to start strategically over-delivering. Pick some moments that matter because in today's world, moments are more important than you could possibly imagine. You win people in micro moments on their journey. Personalize it. Find ways to customize the journey for them. As Russell was talking about this prime mover, I'm thinking, oh, my gosh, how personalized this is going to be? You can show up and you can actually go to the trains whenever it's good for you. That's awesome. You can have coaching calls and all-fast hours whenever you need it. It's going to help you and they're going to know you and they're going to understand it. Find ways to gamify their experience. What do I mean by gamify? Find ways to actually reward and recognize people. Here's the thing. People will do what you pay them to do, but people love doing what you reward and recognize them for doing. And there's a big difference between them just doing it and then loving doing it. So find ways to reward and recognize people. And create rituals. So I got asked to speak at this event in Key West last year and I go to this thing and there's this evening party. I didn't know anybody. It was actually a bunch of mattress store owners. And it was called Dream Camp. And so I'm there and the guy that's hosting it has a cocktail reception. And so I'm sitting at this cocktail reception and all of a sudden I hear this noise. It's like a clank of a coin. I look around and all of a sudden people start slapping coins down on tables. And then some guy starts like dropping down the floor doing push-ups. Another guy starts saying, and don't stop believing by journey. And I'm like, where the heck am I? What kind of crazy group is this? And what they had, they all had this crazy coin. Right? And this coin was part of this ritual. They all loved it. They're coin holders. They feel connected to each other. The rule is you have to have the coin or you have to pay the fine. Push-ups or sitting in a journey in front of everybody. Right? And so if they meet each other in an airport, out in the wilderness, they have to have their coin. They show their coins to each other. There's a code that this coin represents. Like one of the things that I learned that night is that you're not allowed to get your own drink. Coin holders get drinks for other people because it encourages a different culture. Some of the important rituals, people love rituals. People love coming back to Final Hacking Live because of the rituals and the things that go on. Find ways in your business to create rituals. And my last one for you tonight is be unreasonable. What do I mean by that? I mean, ask yourself this question. What could I do for my customers that would be so unreasonably awesome? They would blow their mind. They would be a customer for life. They would never, ever, ever think of leaving us. Now it may scare you. You might start to think, oh my gosh, how could I afford it? Don't go there. Do the exercise. Play the game. Think about it. Because when you start to figure it out, amazing things happen. I'm going to give you an example and a final story. So I watched this unreasonable thinking in action. There was this thing that JetBlue did. Anybody a JetBlue fan? I'm a United Airlines guy. But JetBlue knew that they had to do something cool. So they did this promotion where they had this flight going from JFK all the way over to Orans County to Disneyland. And they had a million babies on this plane. And if you've ever been on a plane and you have the screaming kids around you, you want to strangle them. Babies are cute. And I've had the kids. And you feel bad as a parent that has the kids too. But you get stuck. And so they decided that they were going to do this unreasonably awesome thing. So every single time a baby cried during the flight, they gave everybody 25% off their next flight. And if you got four cries during the flight, you got a free ticket. And the slogan was, next time, smile at a baby for crying out loud. Now it's an amazing video. I don't have time to show it to you. If you want to see the video, I put it on my website. You can grab that. No opt-ins. I just want you to see what it looks like. It's an amazing video. It's an unreasonable approach. It turned people that hated and experienced that they were so unhappy that it could happen to them into people that loved the airline. And at the end, you'll watch the video. People were cheering for the baby. They were hoping that fourth baby was going to cry. How can you do that in your business? We got to get the F out. Focus on your customers. Put the glasses on as if you were them. And start paying attention to obsessing over getting the results. Russell shared a quote from Myron about obsessing over the transformation, not the transaction. It's time to do that on both sides of the phone. Thank you, everybody. All right. We did it.
